content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 8, 1991

Herbert Lobell
President
Mendocino City Community Services District
Post Office Box 1029
Mendocino, CA 95460

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-145

Dear Mr. Lobell:

This letter is in reply to your request for advice regarding
your duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Your request
does not address a specific decision pending before your agency
but rather seeks general guidance regarding multiple future
decisions. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329
(copy enclosed).2

QUESTIONS

1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting
real property on which you hold a second deed of trust?

2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit your participation in decisions affecting a source of
income or promised income?

_________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit
your participation in governmental decisions affecting real
property on which you hold a second deed of trust if it is
reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, upon the real property.

2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit
your participation in governmental decisions affecting a source of
income or promised income.

FACTS

You are a member of the Mendocino City Community Services
District (the "district") and presently its chairperson. The
district is in the process of installing a community water system.

You hold a second deed of trust on the Whitegate Inn, a five-
room "Bed and Breakfast." The value of the mortgage is
approximately $120,000. It is your belief that the installation
of a community water system will increase the value of the inn.
However, your interest in the real property would not increase or
decrease.

You seek our advice to determine whether you may participate
in governmental decisions regarding the installation of the
community water system.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest. As a member of the Mendocino City
Community Services District, you are a public official. (Section
82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of the official's immediate family,³ or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending

3 An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 3

     institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     Section 87103(b) and (c), emphasis added.

     For purposes of the Act, an interest in real property
includes a deed of trust held on property as security on a note if
the fair market value of the interest is $1,000 or more. (Section
82033; Phillips Advice Letter, No. I-90-340, copy enclosed.) You
hold a second deed of trust valued at approximately $120,000.
Accordingly, you may not participate in any governmental decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect of the
decision on the public generally, upon your interest in real
property.

     You have not advised us whether you are receiving payments on
this note. We caution, however, that even if you have not
received in excess of $250 in the preceding twelve months, the
payor is also a disqualifying source of promised income to you
under Section 87103(c). Accordingly, you must disqualify yourself
from participating in governmental decisions which will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on this source of income. (Phillips, supra.)

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

     As a public official, you make a governmental decision or
participate in the making of a governmental decision when you
vote on a matter, commit your agency to a course of action, or
enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of your agency.
(Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.) Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

     (1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

     (2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

     (A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of judg-
ment on the part of the official or designated
employee and the purpose of which is to influence
the decision; or

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 4


     (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.

                                    Regulation 18700(c).

     Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your
interest in real property or your source of income will be materially
affected by decisions regarding the community water system, you must
not only disqualify yourself from participating in formal decisions
which may affect such interests, but you must also abstain from
attempting to influence such decisions by communicating with other
members of the board of directors of the Mendocino City Community
Services District or with the staff regarding such decisions.

Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.  To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required.  (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).)  The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflict of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest.  (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

     You have stated that the community water system will increase
the value of the inn.  It is also foreseeable that the community
water system will have an effect on the assets and liabilities of
the person who is a source of promised income to you as the value
of the inn increases.  Therefore, our analysis must turn now to
the issue of whether the effect of decisions related to the
community water system will be material.

Materiality

     a.  Real property interest

     Regulation 18702 (copy enclosed) sets forth the guidelines
for determining whether an official's economic interest in a
decision is materially affected by a decision.  If the official's
economic interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation
18702.1 (copy enclosed) provides the appropriate standard for
assessing materiality.  If official's interests in real
property are indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the
appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.3 (copy enclosed).

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 5

An official's interest in real property is directly involved
in a governmental decision if:

(A) The decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of real property in which
the official has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or
more, or a similar decision affecting such
property;

(B) The decision involves the issuance,
denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific
use or uses of such property;

(C) The decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees
assessed or imposed on such property; or

(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to the act
of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or development
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category.

Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).

The factors listed in Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) do not appear
to apply to your facts. Accordingly, it is unlikely that the
decisions made by the district will have a direct effect on your
interest in real property.

If the pending decisions regarding the community water system
do not involve the Whitegate Inn directly, we determine
materiality by applying the standards set forth in Regulation

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 6

18702.3 which states in pertinent part that the effect of a
decision is material for real property indirectly involved in a
decision if:

     The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

                         Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).

     Based on the information provided, it would appear that
decisions regarding community water services will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which you have a
financial interest. The pending decisions involve the
installation of a community water system. Construction of the
improvements will result in new or substantially improved services
to the Whitegate Inn in which you have a financial interest. If
this is so, you must disqualify yourself from participating in
such decisions unless the "public generally" exception applies, as
discussed below.

     b.  Source of income

     The appropriate standard for determining materiality when a
source of income that is an individual is affected indirectly by a
governmental decision is that of Regulation 18702.6 (copy
enclosed) which states that a decision is material if any of the
following applies:

          (a) The decision will affect the individual's
     income, investments, or other tangible or
     intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
     property) by $1,000 or more; or

          (b) The decision will affect the individual's
     real property interest in a manner that is
     considered material under Section 18702.3 or
     Section 18702.4.

                         Regulation 18702.6.

     We have insufficient information to determine materiality.
Please refer to Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4 (copies enclosed)
to reach this determination. Additionally, if the payor on the
second deed of trust is a business entity, materiality is
determined by applying the standards of Regulation 18702.2 (copy
enclosed).

     Public generally

     Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the

Our File No. I-91-145
Page 7

effect on your economic interest is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally, or a significant segment of the
public. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703; In re Legan (1985) 9
FPPC Ops. 1, copies enclosed.) The "public" consists of the
entire jurisdiction of the agency. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.
77, copy enclosed.)

It would appear that the installation of a community water
system would be beneficial to all the residents of the district.
However, you have stated that installation of a community water
system will increase the value of the inn. This increase in value
may be substantially different from the increase in value realized
by single-family residences. In order for the "public generally"
exception to apply, we must compare the effect of the decision on
your interest in real property and on your source of income with
the effect of the decision on persons similarly situated. (Larsen
Advice Letter, No. I-91-085, copy enclosed.) We have insufficient
information to make this determination.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Bianca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting real property on which you hold a second deed of trust?

2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in decisions affecting a source of income or promised income?

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
sections.conclusion:
1. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting real property on which you hold a second deed of trust if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, upon the real property.

2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting a source of income or promised income.
sections.facts:
You are a member of the Mendocino City Community Services District (the "district") and presently its chairperson. The district is in the process of installing a community water system.

You hold a second deed of trust on the Whitegate Inn, a five-room "Bed and Breakfast." The value of the mortgage is approximately $120,000. It is your belief that the installation of a community water system will increase the value of the inn. However, your interest in the real property would not increase or decrease.

You seek our advice to determine whether you may participate in governmental decisions regarding the installation of the community water system.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in, or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest. As a member of the Mendocino City Community Services District, you are a public official. (Section 82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family,3 or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending

3 An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.)

institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(b) and (c), emphasis added.

For purposes of the Act, an interest in real property includes a deed of trust held on property as security on a note if the fair market value of the interest is $1,000 or more. (Section 82033; Phillips Advice Letter, No.: I-90-340, copy enclosed.) You hold a second deed of trust valued at approximately $120,000. Accordingly, you may not participate in any governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally, upon your interest in real property.

You have not advised us whether you are receiving payments on this note. We caution, however, that even if you have not received in excess of $250 in the preceding twelve months, the payor is also a disqualifying source of promised income to you under Section 87103(c). Accordingly, you must disqualify yourself from participating in governmental decisions which will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on this source of income. (Phillips, supra.)

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision

As a public official, you make a governmental decision or participate in the making of a governmental decision whenever you vote on a matter, commit your agency to a course of action, or enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of your agency. (Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.) Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your interest in real property or your source of income or promised income will be materially affected by decisions regarding the community water system, you must not only disqualify yourself from participating in formal decisions which may affect such interests, but you must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions by communicating with other members of the board of directors of the Mendocino City Community Services District or with the staff regarding such decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility; however certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

You have stated that the community water system will increase the value of the inn. It is also foreseeable that the community water system will have an effect on the assets and liabilities of the person who is a source of promised income to you as the value of the inn increases. Therefore, our analysis must turn now to the issue of whether the effect of decisions related to the community water system will be material.

Materiality

a. Real property interest

Regulation 18702 (copy enclosed) sets forth the guidelines for determining whether an official's economic interest in a decision is materially affected by a decision. If the official's economic interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality. If an official's interests in real property are indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.3 (copy enclosed).

An official's interest in real property is directly involved in a governmental decision if:

(A) The decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest (other than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or more, or a similar decision affecting such property;

(B) The decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of such property;

(C) The decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on such property; or

(D) The decision is to designate the survey area, to select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use designation on the subject property, but shall not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the uses permitted, or development standards applicable, within a particular zoning category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that category.

Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).

The factors listed in Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) do not appear to apply to your facts. Accordingly, it is unlikely that the decisions made by the district will have a direct effect on your interest in real property.

If the pending decisions regarding the community water system do not involve the Whitegate Inn directly, we determine materiality by applying the standards set forth in Regulation

18702.3 which states in pertinent part that the effect of a decision is material for real property indirectly involved in a decision if:

    The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or substantially improved services.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).

Based on the information provided, it would appear that decisions regarding community water services will have a material financial effect on the real property in which you have a financial interest. The pending decisions involve the installation of a community water system. Construction of the improvements will result in new or substantially improved services to the Whitegate Inn in which you have a financial interest. If this is so, you must disqualify yourself from participating in such decisions unless the "public generally" exception applies, as discussed below.

b. Source of income

The appropriate standard for determining materiality when a source of income that is an individual is affected indirectly by a governmental decision is that of Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) which states that a decision is material if any of the following applies:

    (a) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or

    (b) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

Regulation 18702.6.

We have insufficient information to determine materiality. Please refer to Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4 (copies enclosed) to reach this determination. Additionally, if the payor on the second deed of trust is a business entity, materiality is determined by applying the standards of Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed).

Public generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is material, disqualification is required only if the

effect on your economic interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, or a significant segment of the public. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703; In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, copies enclosed.) The "public" consists of the entire jurisdiction of the agency. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copy enclosed.)

It would appear that the installation of a community water system would be beneficial to all the residents of the district. However, you have stated that installation of a community water system will increase the value of the inn. This increase in value may be substantially different from the increase in value realized by single-family residences. In order for the "public generally" exception to apply, we must compare the effect of the decision on your interest in real property and on your source of income with the effect of the decision on persons similarly situated. (Larsen Advice Letter, No. I-91-085, copy enclosed.) We have insufficient information to make this determination.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

QUESTION: 1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting real property on which you hold a second deed of trust?

2. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in decisions affecting a source of income or promised income?

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

CONCLUSION: 1. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting real property on which you hold a second deed of trust if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, upon the real property.

2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit your participation in governmental decisions affecting a source of income or promised income.
